read.image: a dead friend's name tatted on the arm
read.take: Everybody's wishing bad things on him, and the answer he gives back isn't a clapback. It's a list of names. He's moving calm, even though keeping it peaceful is a struggle, and under that calm sits something darker. He doesn't want to die just so the world finally misses him, the way it only learns to love its artists once they're gone. So he counts the people who keep him here instead. His bed and his mama. The broskies. 40 and Oli, without whom there'd be no him. And Fif, gone since the fall, whose name he got tatted on his arm so it outlives them both. The loyalty is the armor. Whatever God's got planned, he can't do it on his own, and he says so plainly while he makes sure the north-side eats.
read.contradiction: He doesn't want to die just for the world to finally miss him, yet he keeps circling back to how going down would let him go down a G-O-D.

arc.label: The Wishing
arc.scene: He's moving calm and starting nothing, but he can feel the bad things people are wishing on him from every side. The calm is a struggle to hold. Already death is in his mouth — he hopes he's got brothers who outlive him, and he doesn't want to be missed only after he's gone.

arc.label: The Answer
arc.scene: God's plan, he says, like that settles it. He holds back, and sometimes he won't. The reply to all that hate isn't aimed back at anyone — it's making sure the north-side eats and that he goes down for something different. And still.

arc.label: The Roll Call
arc.scene: When she asks if he loves her, he only loves her partly. His bed and his mama come first, and he's sorry. Then he names the rest of the people holding him up — Fif tatted on his skin, 40 and Oli, the broskies he can't imagine never meeting. He can't do this on his own, and he doesn't pretend he could.

lines.line: Hope I got some brothers that outlive me
I make sure that north-side eat
I only love my bed and my mama, I'm sorry
Without 40, Oli, there'd be no me

read.texture: A slow, muted beat at a crawl, low-lit and sparse, the kind of bounce that keeps you nodding without ever brightening. The groove is there to dance to, but the color is drained out of it, the lift carrying more weight than it lets on.
